pattern: Since a swap can pair up any two positions at all, the final arrangement only needs some zero to be sitting somewhere in the trailing block, so the answer is just how many zeros are still stuck in the leading block that must eventually hold only non-zero values.
hints: Because a swap can exchange any two positions (not just neighbors), the order among the non-zero values never has to change at all.

Split the array into a leading block sized to hold every non-zero value and a trailing block sized to hold every zero; each stray zero sitting in the leading block needs exactly one swap to trade places with a stray non-zero in the trailing block.
Count how many zeros currently sit inside that leading block — that count is both the minimum number of swaps and, equivalently, the number of non-zero values currently sitting in the trailing block.

edge_inputs.description: a single zero, nothing to move it past
zeros already sitting entirely at the end, so no swap is needed
exactly one zero out of place, right at the split point

stress.description: random arrays with a variable mix of zero and non-zero values
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100))
    nums = [rng.randint(0, 100) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
